Hello Sam. Welcome back. It's good to be here with you again, as we continue this important work. I know you're engaging with some deep and complex material... and that takes courage. Today, we're going to explore some concepts around how past experiences can shape our present... and how we can gently begin to reshape those patterns. This is about creating more space and choice in your life. We'll move slowly, with care.

First, let's just settle in for a moment. Find a position that feels supportive for your body... whether you're sitting or lying down. Allow your eyes to close if that feels comfortable... or simply soften your gaze. And just notice the contact points of your body... the places where you are supported by the chair or the floor. There's nothing you need to change right now... just this simple noticing.

Now, I'd like to invite you to bring a gentle awareness to your breathing. Don't try to control it... just follow the natural rhythm. Feel the air as it enters your nostrils... cool and light. Feel the slight expansion in your chest or belly. And then feel the warmer air as it leaves your body on the exhale. Just tracking this cycle... in... and out. This breath is an anchor... a place you can return to at any time.

Sometimes, our minds develop very efficient patterns based on what we've experienced. The mind learns to protect us... to anticipate danger... or to manage overwhelming feelings. These patterns are like well-worn trails in a forest. They become the automatic route we take. And while they served a purpose once... they might not lead where we want to go now. It's important to recognize this with kindness. Your mind was doing its job to keep you safe.

What we can practice is noticing the trail without immediately walking down it. This is a skill of observation. So let's try that now. As you continue to breathe, see if you can notice the next thought that arises. Don't judge it... don't push it away... don't get pulled into its story. Just acknowledge it. You might silently say to yourself, "Ah, there's a thought." Or, "I notice planning." Or, "I notice remembering." Then, gently return your attention to the feeling of your next breath. The thought may come back... that's okay. Just notice it again... and return to the anchor. This isn't about emptying the mind. It's about changing your relationship to the activity of the mind.

We can extend this same gentle noticing to emotions and physical sensations. Often, they are linked. See if you can scan through your body now... from the crown of your head down to your toes. Move your attention slowly. Are there places of tension... or warmth... or numbness? Just note them. "Tightness in the shoulders." "A feeling of heaviness here." There's no need to fix anything. Your only task is to map the territory of your present moment experience... with curiosity instead of criticism.

Now, a core part of cognitive restructuring is examining the stories we tell ourselves. These stories often contain "cognitive distortions"... which are just habitual ways of thinking that can cause pain. One common pattern is all-or-nothing thinking. Seeing things in black and white categories. Another is emotional reasoning... believing that because we *feel* something is true, it must *be* true. When you notice a painful or persistent thought, you can pause and ask... is this thought completely factual? Or is it a interpretation? Is there another way to see this situation? This questioning isn't about forcing positivity. It's about introducing flexibility... creating a gap between the thought and your reaction to it.

Let's practice with a neutral example. The thought might be, "I made a mistake, so I am a failure." That's a very fused thought. It takes one event and turns it into a global identity. See if you can gently unhook from that. You might say, "I notice I'm having the thought that I am a failure because I made a mistake." Already, that creates a little space. Then you might ask, "What is the evidence for and against this thought?" Making a mistake is evidence of being human... not evidence of being a failure. The practice is to hold both truths at once. Yes, a mistake happened. And no, it does not define your entire worth.

This work often touches on memories or feelings that have been stored for a long time. If something feels too big, too sharp, or too overwhelming during our time together... that is a signal. It is a signal to pause... to come back to your breath... to open your eyes and look around the room. Name five things you can see. Feel the solid ground beneath you. You are here, in the present. The memory or feeling is not happening now... even though it feels vivid. This is a crucial distinction. You can acknowledge the past experience... honor the feelings that came with it... without being retraumatized by it in this moment. You have more tools now. You have this breath. You have this practice of noticing.

Let's try a grounding technique that uses the senses. Keep your eyes open for this. Look around you and find one object. Study it as if you've never seen it before. Notice its color... its texture... its shape. Is it hard or soft? Shiny or matte? Now, reach out and touch it if you can. Feel its temperature... its surface. Just be with this object for a few moments... letting it draw your attention fully into the here and now. Good.

When processing difficult material, the goal is integration... not eradication. We are not trying to delete memories or pretend they didn't happen. We are trying to change their emotional charge... and the power they have over your present life. Imagine you have a library inside you. For a long time, certain books were on a high shelf, gathering dust. They felt too dangerous to touch. The work of processing is like taking one of those books down... with great care. You don't have to read the whole book today. Maybe you just look at the cover. You see that it's a book... not the actual event. You realize you can place it back on the shelf... but now on a lower shelf, where you can see it. It becomes part of your history... without being the dictator of your present.

This is deep work, Sam. It requires patience and immense self-compassion. There will be days when the old trails feel like the only paths. On those days, the practice is simply to notice you are on that trail... and to be kind to yourself for being there. The act of noticing is the first step toward choosing a different direction.

Let's finish with a few minutes of rest. Allow everything we've touched on to simply be. There's no more analyzing to do right now. Come back to the simple rhythm of your breath. In... and out. Feel your body fully supported. You are safe in this moment. You have done a brave thing by showing up for this work. Remember, these audio sessions are a supportive tool... but they are not a substitute for working with a qualified therapist, especially when dealing with trauma. If things feel unmanageable, reaching out to a professional is a profound act of strength.

For now, just breathe. And when you're ready, gently bring some small movement back to your fingers and toes. Notice the space around you. And carry this sense of gentle awareness with you as you move into the rest of your day. You are building something new, one breath, one noticed thought, at a time.

You know, sometimes our feelings are like the weather inside our bodies. One minute it can be sunny and calm... and the next minute, a big storm cloud of worry or a gust of angry wind might blow in. That storm can make our hearts beat fast... our tummy feel funny... or our thoughts race around like leaves in the wind. That's just what happens sometimes. Trace up your middle finger... breathe in slowly. And trace down... letting the breath go. Now your ring finger... tracing up with an inhale. Tracing down with a long exhale. Let's do that one more time on the same hand... going even slower this time. Trace up your thumb... breathe in... two... three... four... five. Trace down... breathe out... two... three... four... five... six. Follow the edges of each finger... letting your breath be your guide. There's no rush at all. This is your time. If your mind wanders to a thought... that's okay. Just gently bring your attention back to the feeling of your finger on your skin... and the sound of my voice. You're doing great.

Sometimes, big feelings make energy buzz in our bodies. It's like we have a bunch of buzzing bees inside. So let's play a game to help that energy move in a calm way. This is called Melt and Freeze. First, let's make our whole body as tense and frozen as a statue. Squeeze everything. Make your fists really tight... scrunch up your face... squeeze your toes... make your legs and arms stiff. Hold it... hold it... and... melt. Let everything go completely loose and floppy. Like a rag doll. Let your head drop... let your arms dangle. Feel all that tightness just melt away into the floor. Good. Let's do it two more times. Freeze. Be the tightest, most frozen statue you can be. Squeeze all those buzzing bees into a ball. And... melt. Let them all go... floating away. One last time. Freeze everything. And now... melt into total calm. Wonderful. Notice how your body feels now. Softer. Heavier. More quiet inside.

Now, let's go back to your imagination. We're going to visit your cozy place one more time... but this time, we're bringing a friend. Imagine one of your big feelings is visiting you there. So here's something you can practice this week. Maybe you can ask a grown-up to do the hand tracing with you. You can trace on their hand... and they can trace on yours. But always, always talk to the grown-ups who love you about how you're feeling. They are your best team.

You are so brave for exploring your feelings like this. You are learning the superpower of calm... and that is a truly wonderful thing. I'm so proud of you. Be gentle with yourself. I'll see you next time.

Hello there. It's so good to be with you today. I want to welcome you to this quiet space, a little pocket of time just for you. I know that sometimes feelings can feel really big and confusing, like a storm inside. That's okay. Lots of people feel that way. Today, we're going to explore a few simple, gentle ways to help those big feelings settle. We'll use our imagination and our breath, like tools for a quiet adventure. So get comfortable... wherever you are... and let's begin.

First, let's just notice where we are right now. You don't have to change anything. Just notice. Can you feel your body resting against the chair or the floor? Can you feel the air on your skin? Just noticing. Good. Now, let's take one breath together, just to arrive here. A slow breath in through your nose... and a gentle sigh out through your mouth. There. You've already begun.

Sometimes our minds are like a busy beehive, full of buzzing thoughts and feelings. They zoom around and it can feel loud and crowded in there. That's a normal thing that happens. It's not your fault. Our minds are just doing their job, trying to keep us safe and figure things out. But we can learn to watch the bees instead of getting stung by them. We can learn to watch our thoughts and feelings from a little distance, like watching clouds drift across the sky. A thought is just a thought. A feeling is just a feeling. It comes... and then, if we let it, it goes.

So let's try that now. Let's play a game of cloud watching, but on the inside. Close your eyes if that feels okay. If not, just soften your gaze. Now, imagine the sky of your mind. It's a big, beautiful, blue sky. And into that sky comes a thought. Maybe it's a thought like, "I'm worried." Picture that thought taking the shape of a cloud. It might be a dark cloud or a puffy white one. Just see it there. Now, watch what the cloud does. Does it sit still? Does it slowly drift from one side of your sky to the other? You don't have to push the cloud away. You don't have to make it bigger. Your only job is to notice it. "Ah, there's a worry cloud." Good. Now, see if you can let it drift... all on its own. Watch it move across your inner sky until it passes out of view. Beautiful.

Now another cloud might float in. Maybe it's a feeling of frustration. Give that feeling a cloud shape too. Is it a jagged cloud? A heavy, gray cloud? Just notice it. Name it quietly to yourself. "Frustration is here." And again, just watch it. See if you can watch it without getting on the cloud and riding it. Just stay on the ground, watching the sky. Let the cloud do its thing. It will drift along... and eventually, it will drift away. Sometimes a whole bunch of clouds come at once. That's okay too. Just notice the whole cloudy sky. The sky itself is always there, behind all the clouds. Your calm, quiet awareness is like that sky. The clouds of thought and feeling are always changing, but the sky remains.

Let's take a deep breath together... in... and out. Good.

Now, let's bring our attention down from the sky and into our bodies. Our bodies are amazing. They can give us clues about how we're feeling and they can also help us feel more steady. Let's try a practice called "anchoring." An anchor is something that holds a boat steady, even when the water is wavy. We can find an anchor inside our own body.

Start by finding your hands. Bring your attention to your hands. Feel your palms. Feel your fingers. Now, slowly press your palms together. Press them firmly, like you're giving yourself a gentle, strong handshake. Feel the pressure in your hands and your fingers. Hold that pressure for a moment... and then slowly release. Feel the sensation as you let go. Notice the tingling, the warmth. Good. Now, do that again. Press your palms together firmly. Feel that solid, steady feeling. This is your anchor. You are here. You are solid. Now slowly release... and feel the softness in your hands. You can do this anytime you need to feel more grounded. Just press your palms together and remember, "I am here. I am steady."

Let's connect this anchor to our breath. Place one hand gently on your chest, right over your heart. Feel the warmth of your hand through your shirt. Now, take a slow breath in... and feel your chest rise gently under your hand. Hold it for just a second... and then breathe out slowly, feeling your chest fall. Let's do that again. Breathe in slowly... feeling the gentle rise. And breathe out even slower... feeling the gentle fall. Your hand is an anchor of kindness, right over your heart. With every breath in, imagine you're breathing in calm. With every breath out, imagine you're breathing out any tightness or worry. In for calm... out for release. In... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five...

Wonderful. You're doing so well.

Now, I want to share a little story with you. Imagine you have a special backpack. This isn't a backpack for school books. This is your feeling backpack. Throughout the day, we sometimes pick up heavy rocks of worry, or sticky burrs of frustration, and we put them in our backpack without even noticing. By the end of the day, the backpack can feel so heavy. Our shoulders get tired. Our heart feels weighed down.

So let's do a practice of unpacking that backpack. Get comfortable again. Close your eyes. Imagine you're taking off that heavy backpack and setting it down in front of you. You unzip the top. Inside, you might see some of those heavy rocks. One rock might be named "The Thing I'm Anxious About." See it. You don't have to throw it away. Just take it out of the backpack and set it on the ground beside you. Good. Now reach in again. Maybe you find a prickly burr named "Annoyance." Take that out too. Set it down. Go through your backpack, item by item. "Sadness." Take it out. "Tiredness." Take it out. Just set each one on the ground. You are not getting rid of your feelings. You are simply taking them out of the heavy bag you've been carrying. You are giving them some space to just be, without the weight. When you've taken out a few of the heavier items, imagine putting your backpack back on. Notice how much lighter it feels. Your shoulders feel freer. Your breath comes a little easier. You can still see all those feelings sitting beside you. They are there. But you are not carrying their full weight on your back anymore. You can just let them be.

Take a deep breath in... and a long breath out. Very good.

Let's come back to our breath for a few more moments, making it just a little bit more playful. This is called "Bunny Breath." It's a fun way to wake up our energy and focus. Imagine you're a curious little bunny, sniffing the air. Take three quick, little sniffs in through your nose... sniff, sniff, sniff. Then one long, slow breath out through your mouth... like a sigh. Let's try it. Sniff in... sniff in... sniff in... and loooong sigh out. Good. One more time. Quick sniffs in... and a slow, relaxing breath out. Isn't it funny how changing our breath can change how we feel, even just a little bit?

Now, let's try a practice of gratitude, which is just a fancy word for noticing one good thing. It doesn't have to be huge. It can be very small. Think of one thing right now that feels okay, or that you appreciate. Maybe it's the feeling of being comfortable where you're sitting. Maybe it's a memory of someone smiling at you. Maybe it's the taste of your last meal. Find one small thing. Got it? Now, just hold that thought in your mind for ten seconds. Let it fill up your attention. Imagine that good feeling warming you up from the inside, like a little sun in your heart. Breathe into that feeling. Breathe in... and out. Good.

You have been so brave and patient in exploring all of this with me. You've watched the clouds in your sky. You've anchored yourself with your hands and your breath. You've unpacked a heavy backpack. And you've noticed one good thing. These are all tools for your toolkit. You can use any of them, anytime.

So as we finish up, I want you to remember just one thing. The next time you notice a big feeling, you can simply pause... and take one breath. Just one slow, kind breath. That single breath is a powerful way to create a little space between you and the feeling. In that space, you can choose what to do next. You can press your palms together. You can watch the thought like a cloud. You can be your own anchor.

You are learning how to be a kind friend to yourself. And that is truly wonderful. Thank you for spending this time with me. Be gentle with yourself today. You're doing great. Goodbye for now.
